sentiment.rationale: A strong rebuke of Israel's sinfulness, likening them to Sodom and Gomorrah, with a call to repentance and cleansing.
Opens with a glorious vision of the latter-day temple and peace among nations, then warns against pride and idolatry.
Pronounces judgment on Jerusalem and Judah, removing their leaders and condemning the haughty daughters of Zion.
A messianic promise of the Branch of the Lord, cleansing of Zion, and divine protection with cloud and fire.
The parable of the vineyard and a series of woes against greed, drunkenness, and moral inversion.
Isaiah's throne-room vision of the Lord's glory, his cleansing, and his prophetic commission.
The Immanuel prophecy amid Ahaz's fear, with warnings of Assyrian invasion as consequence of faithlessness.
Prophesies Assyria's coming flood, warns against trusting in conspiracies, and urges binding the testimony among disciples.
The great messianic prophecy of the child born, the Prince of Peace, whose government shall have no end.
Woe upon Assyria as God's instrument of judgment, followed by the promise of a remnant returning to the mighty God.
The Branch from Jesse's stem, filled with the Spirit, ushering in a millennial era of peace and the gathering of Israel.
A short psalm of thanksgiving and praise for God's salvation, joy, and the proclamation of His works among the nations.
The burden of Babylon — cosmic destruction imagery, the day of the Lord's wrath, and Babylon's total desolation.
The fall of Lucifer and the king of Babylon, with Israel's restoration and a burden against Philistia.
A lament over the destruction of Moab, with weeping, mourning, and desolation across its cities.
Continues the burden of Moab with a plea for refuge and mercy, ending with sorrow over Moab's vineyards and pride.
The burden of Damascus and Ephraim — judgment upon both, with a brief turning to the Holy One of Israel.
An oracle concerning Ethiopia, with imagery of God quietly watching, and a future offering brought to Mount Zion.
The burden of Egypt — internal strife, divine judgment, but concluding with Egypt and Assyria worshipping God alongside Israel.
Isaiah walks naked and barefoot as a sign of Egypt and Ethiopia's coming captivity by Assyria.
Visions of Babylon's fall, burdens upon Dumah and Arabia, with watchman imagery of approaching judgment.
The burden of the valley of vision — Jerusalem's reckless revelry in the face of invasion, and the replacement of Shebna by Eliakim.
The burden of Tyre — weeping over the destruction of the great merchant city, with a seventy-year desolation.
An apocalyptic vision of earth's desolation and judgment, with brief praise from the ends of the earth amid terror.
A triumphant song praising God for destroying the oppressor, swallowing up death, and wiping away all tears.
A song of trust in the Lord as an everlasting rock, with the promise of resurrection and perfect peace for the steadfast.
The slaying of leviathan, the care of God's vineyard, and the eventual gathering of Israel with the trumpet.
Woe to the drunkards of Ephraim and the scornful rulers of Jerusalem, with the cornerstone prophecy amid warnings.
Woe to Ariel (Jerusalem), the sealed book prophecy, and the promise that the meek shall increase their joy in the Lord.
Woe to rebellious children trusting in Egypt; yet God waits to be gracious, and promises guidance and blessing to the obedient.
Woe to those who rely on Egyptian horses instead of the Holy One; God will defend Jerusalem like birds hovering.
A righteous king shall reign, and after desolation, the Spirit is poured out bringing justice, peace, and quietness forever.
Woe to the treacherous; yet the Lord is exalted and fills Zion with justice, and the redeemed see the King in His beauty.

summaries.summary: God calls heaven and earth to witness Israel's rebellion, rebukes empty rituals, and invites the nation to repentance with the promise that sins can become white as snow.
A vision of the latter-day temple exalted above the hills with nations flowing to it, followed by warnings against pride and idolatry in the day of the Lord.
The Lord removes Judah's leaders and provisions as judgment, the people oppress one another, and the haughty daughters of Zion are stripped of their finery.
In that day the Branch of the Lord shall be beautiful, Zion's remnant shall be called holy, and God will create a cloud and fire over every dwelling for protection.
The parable of the Lord's vineyard that yielded wild grapes, followed by six woes against greed, drunkenness, moral confusion, and pride.
Isaiah sees the Lord upon His throne, his lips are cleansed by a seraph's coal, and he receives his prophetic commission to preach to a people who will not understand.
During the Syro-Ephraimite crisis, Isaiah reassures King Ahaz, delivers the Immanuel sign, and prophesies Assyria's devastating invasion of Judah.
Isaiah names his son Maher-shalal-hash-baz as a sign of Assyria's swift conquest, warns against trusting conspiracies, and urges the people to seek God's law and testimony.
Light shines on those in darkness with the birth of a child called Wonderful Counselor, Prince of Peace, yet God's anger continues against an unrepentant Israel.
Woe to unjust lawmakers; Assyria is God's rod of anger but will itself be judged for arrogance, and a remnant of Israel shall return to the mighty God.
A rod from Jesse's stem brings righteous judgment, the wolf lies with the lamb in millennial peace, and the Lord gathers scattered Israel a second time.
A brief hymn of thanksgiving praising God for His salvation, declaring His doings among the people, and rejoicing because the Holy One of Israel is great.
The burden of Babylon — the Lord musters armies for the day of His wrath, cosmic signs attend the destruction, and Babylon is left utterly desolate.
Israel is restored and takes up a taunt against the fallen king of Babylon; Lucifer's fall from heaven is described, and burdens are pronounced upon Philistia.
A lament over the swift destruction of Moab's cities, with refugees fleeing and weeping across the devastated land.
Moab is urged to send tribute and seek shelter; its pride is condemned, and Isaiah weeps over the desolation of Moab's vineyards and joy.
The burden of Damascus and Ephraim — both are destined for ruin, though a remnant shall regard the Holy One of Israel in that day.
An oracle to the land beyond Ethiopia's rivers; God watches quietly as events unfold, and eventually a people shall bring offerings to Mount Zion.
The burden of Egypt — internal strife, drought, and a foolish counsel, yet in that day Egypt, Assyria, and Israel shall worship together as God's blessed people.
Isaiah walks naked and barefoot for three years as a sign that Assyria will lead Egypt and Ethiopia away captive.
Visions of Babylon's fall at the hands of Media and Elam, a brief oracle concerning Dumah, and a burden upon Arabia's caravans.
The valley of vision — Jerusalem revels instead of repenting during siege; Shebna the steward is removed and Eliakim is set in his place with authority.
The burden of Tyre — the great merchant city is destroyed, lies forgotten for seventy years, then returns to commerce with its profits consecrated to the Lord.
An apocalyptic vision of the earth made empty and desolate for breaking the everlasting covenant; the Lord reigns in glory on Mount Zion before His ancients.
A song of praise for God's marvelous works, the feast of fat things on this mountain, the swallowing up of death, and the wiping away of tears from all faces.
A song of trust in the Lord as an everlasting rock, a vision of perfect peace for the steadfast mind, and the promise of resurrection for the dead.
The Lord punishes leviathan, tenderly guards His vineyard Israel, and in that day a great trumpet gathers the outcasts to worship in Jerusalem.
Woe to Ephraim's drunkards and Jerusalem's scornful rulers; God lays a precious cornerstone in Zion, and His work proceeds line upon line, precept upon precept.
Woe to Ariel — Jerusalem shall be besieged, the vision becomes a sealed book, yet the deaf shall hear and the meek shall increase their joy in the Holy One.
Woe to rebellious children who trust in Egypt's shadow; God waits to be gracious, promises to guide His people, and will destroy Assyria with His voice.
Woe to those relying on Egypt's horses; the Lord will come down to fight for Mount Zion and defend Jerusalem as birds flying overhead.
A righteous king shall reign in justice, complacent women are warned of desolation, and the Spirit poured from on high transforms the wilderness into fruitful peace.
Woe to the spoiler who has not been spoiled; the Lord rises in majesty, fills Zion with justice and righteousness, and the redeemed behold the King in His beauty.

crossReferences.note: The invitation to reason together and be cleansed from sin parallels the call to repent and not procrastinate.
The mountain of the Lord's house established in the top of the mountains, with nations flowing unto it.
Nephi's quotation of Isaiah's throne-room vision in the Book of Mormon.
The Immanuel prophecy of a virgin bearing a son parallels Nephi's vision of the mother of the Son of God.
The messianic prophecy of the child born, Wonderful Counselor, Prince of Peace, quoted directly by Nephi.
The stem of Jesse identified in latter-day revelation as Christ and the priesthood connection.
The fall of Lucifer parallels the vision of the sons of perdition and Satan's pre-mortal rebellion.
The earth defiled by inhabitants who have broken the everlasting covenant parallels the declaration of universal apostasy.
Line upon line, precept upon precept — the pattern of progressive revelation.
The sealed book echoes the episode of Martin Harris taking the characters to Professor Anthon.

verses.reason: 'Come now, and let us reason together'—the iconic invitation to repentance and cleansing from sin.
Promise that the willing and obedient shall eat the good of the land.
The mountain of the Lord's house established in the top of the mountains in the last days.
Swords beaten into plowshares, nations learning war no more.
The righteous are promised it shall be well with them, for they shall eat the fruit of their doings.
The Lord creates a cloud by day and fire by night over every dwelling in Zion.
'Woe unto them that call evil good, and good evil'—a classic warning against moral inversion.
The seraphim's cry: 'Holy, holy, holy, is the LORD of hosts: the whole earth is full of his glory.'
'Here am I; send me'—Isaiah's response to God's call, widely quoted on willingness to serve.
'A virgin shall conceive, and bear a son, and shall call his name Immanuel'—foundational messianic prophecy.
'To the law and to the testimony: if they speak not according to this word, it is because there is no light in them.'
'For unto us a child is born... Wonderful, Counsellor, The mighty God, The everlasting Father, The Prince of Peace.'
'The remnant shall return, even the remnant of Jacob, unto the mighty God.'
'A rod out of the stem of Jesse'—messianic prophecy of Christ's lineage.
'The wolf also shall dwell with the lamb'—iconic millennial peace imagery.
'The LORD JEHOVAH is my strength and my song; he also is become my salvation.'
Cosmic signs—sun, moon, and stars darkened—foreshadowing the day of the Lord.
'How art thou fallen from heaven, O Lucifer, son of the morning!'—the iconic fall of Satan.
A throne established in mercy with a judge seeking justice—a messianic echo within the Moab oracle.
'Blessed be Egypt my people, and Assyria the work of my hands, and Israel mine inheritance'—a remarkable vision of universal blessing.
'Babylon is fallen, is fallen'—echoed in Revelation 18:2.
'The key of the house of David will I lay upon his shoulder'—echoed in Revelation 3:7 regarding Christ's authority.
'The earth also is defiled under the inhabitants thereof; because they have transgressed the laws, changed the ordinance, broken the everlasting covenant.'
'He will swallow up death in victory; and the Lord GOD will wipe away tears from off all faces.'
'Thou wilt keep him in perfect peace, whose mind is stayed on thee: because he trusteth in thee.'
'Thy dead men shall live, together with my dead body shall they arise'—a resurrection promise.
'The great trumpet shall be blown, and they shall come which were ready to perish'—the gathering of Israel.
'Precept upon precept; line upon line'—the pattern of progressive revelation.
'I lay in Zion for a foundation a stone, a tried stone, a precious corner stone, a sure foundation.'
A voice speaking out of the ground—interpreted as the Book of Mormon coming forth from the earth.
'I will proceed to do a marvellous work among this people, even a marvellous work and a wonder.'
'Thine ears shall hear a word behind thee, saying, This is the way, walk ye in it.'
'As birds flying, so will the LORD of hosts defend Jerusalem; defending also he will deliver it.'
'The work of righteousness shall be peace; and the effect of righteousness quietness and assurance for ever.'
'Thine eyes shall see the king in his beauty: they shall behold the land that is very far off.'
